title: IEC 62443-3-3 to NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Cross-Reference
description: Maps IEC 62443-3-3 System Requirements (SRs) to the corresponding guidance sections in NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 (Guide to Operational Technology Security, 2023). Both are OT security references — IEC 62443 is normative for compliance; NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 provides implementation guidance and references NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls applicable to OT. Malaysian NCII operators may use this mapping to understand how IEC 62443 SL compliance aligns with NIST OT guidance.
verificationNote: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 is a public domain document (May 2023) from the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology. Section references are based on the public document structure. This mapping is a reasoned interpretation. Verify against the current NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 document at nvlpubs.nist.gov before use in formal assessments.
nistDocument: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 — Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security, May 2023
iec62443Reference: IEC 62443-3-3:2013 — Industrial communication networks — Network and system security — System security requirements and security levels
mappingApproach: IEC 62443-3-3 defines 51 SRs across 7 Foundational Requirements (FR1-FR7). NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Section 5 organises countermeasures by NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control family, with OT-specific tailoring guidance. This cross-reference maps each IEC 62443 FR and its key SRs to the corresponding NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 section and NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control family.

mappings.frDescription: All users, software processes, and devices must be uniquely identified and authenticated before accessing the IACS.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 discusses OT-specific IA considerations including: challenges with legacy device authentication, use of compensating controls where strong authentication cannot be implemented natively on field devices, and the role of PAM solutions in bridging OT authentication gaps.

srs.nist80082OTNote: NIST 800-82 recognises that legacy OT systems may not support individual accounts. Compensating control: individual authentication enforced at jump server/PAM layer; shared service accounts used for device connectivity only.

srs.srName: Software process and device identification and authentication
srs.nist80082OTNote: Device certificates for OT components where supported. For legacy devices without certificate capability: MAC-based network access control as compensating control.
Account lifecycle management in OT requires coordination with HR. OT accounts often persist after personnel departures — quarterly OT account access reviews are the NIST 800-82 recommended practice.

Default credential elimination is explicitly called out in NIST 800-82 as the highest-impact low-cost OT security action. Password complexity requirements must be balanced against OT operator usability constraints.

NIST 800-82 recommends: all remote access via jump server or VPN with MFA; session recording for all remote OT access; time-limited sessions; vendor remote access via dedicated gateway with approval workflow.

mappings.frDescription: All permissions and authorisations necessary to perform required OT functions are enforced for users, software processes, and devices on the IACS.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 emphasises that OT RBAC must be designed with operations engineering — security teams alone cannot define appropriate OT roles. Least privilege in OT context means: operators get display + control for their area only; engineers get programming access only when needed; vendors get temporary minimal access.

srs.nist80082OTNote: NIST 800-82 OT-specific guidance: separation of OT operator and OT administrator roles is fundamental. Dual-authorisation controls recommended for safety-critical commands (e.g., SIS bypass actions).

srs.srName: Use control for portable and mobile devices
nist80082Sections: SC-41 Port and I/O Device Access
srs.nist80082OTNote: NIST 800-82 specifically addresses removable media as a primary OT attack vector (Stuxnet). Recommends: whitelist-only USB policy, malware scanning kiosk before OT introduction, prohibition on non-organisation devices in OT areas.
Remote sessions in OT must be terminated after task completion — not left persistent. PAM session recording provides evidence of termination and activity.

OT-specific auditable events include: PLC program upload/download, DCS configuration changes, engineering tool connections, SCADA database modifications. Standard IT audit events (user login/logout, privilege use) also apply.

NACSA s26 (7-year retention) exceeds NIST 800-82 recommended retention. OT log storage must be sized for 7-year retention for Malaysian NCII-designated operators. Write-once storage prevents log tampering.

mappings.frDescription: The integrity of the IACS and its data is protected against unauthorised manipulation.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 addresses OT-specific integrity concerns: PLC/firmware integrity verification, the challenge of patching legacy OT systems without vendor support, compensating controls where patches cannot be applied, and supply chain integrity for OT components.

nist80082Sections: SI-7 Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
srs.nist80082OTNote: OT-specific: application whitelisting on OT Windows systems (application control prevents execution of malicious code even when signatures are not available). Traditional AV has high false-positive risk on OT systems — application control preferred.

Security functionality verification in OT requires non-intrusive methods. Passive OT network monitoring (Claroty/Dragos/Nozomi) provides continuous verification without active scanning that could disrupt OT communications.

nist80082Sections: SI-7 Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
srs.nist80082OTNote: PLC program checksum register: document expected checksum for each PLC program version. Compare against current checksum periodically and after any maintenance event. This is a fundamental OT integrity control that many sites lack.
OT audit logs used in NACSA s26 incident reporting must be integrity-protected. Write-once log storage prevents post-incident log tampering.

mappings.frDescription: Information on communication channels and in data repositories is protected against unauthorised disclosure.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 recognises the tension between OT confidentiality and OT availability — encryption must not introduce latency that disrupts real-time control. Encryption is recommended for: management interfaces, remote access channels, historian-to-IT transfers, and data at rest on OT servers. Real-time control bus encryption requires careful performance testing.

srs.nist80082OTNote: Encryption priority for OT: 1) Remote access channels (always encrypted), 2) IDMZ historian transfers, 3) Management interfaces (HTTPS/SSH). Real-time field bus traffic encryption: evaluate latency impact before implementing.

OT media containing process data, configurations, or programs must be securely disposed of. Hard drives from decommissioned OT systems may contain safety-critical configurations — degaussing or physical destruction required.

OT cryptography constraints: many field devices use weak or proprietary cryptography. NIST 800-82 recommends using vendor-supported encryption where available; compensating controls (physical isolation, VPN at perimeter) where field device encryption is not available.

mappings.frDescription: Data flows between different zones and conduits within the IACS are restricted to only those required for the functioning of the IACS.
mappings.nist80082Chapter: Section 4 — OT System Architecture and Section 5 Countermeasures
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 provides detailed OT network architecture guidance: Purdue model implementation, IDMZ design, unidirectional gateways for highest-criticality zones, and firewall rule management for OT. Chapter 4 covers OT architecture patterns in detail.

srs.nist80082OTNote: NIST 800-82 recommends the IDMZ (Industrial DMZ) as the standard OT/IT boundary architecture. No direct connections between IT and OT networks — all data exchange via IDMZ-hosted services (historians, file transfer servers).

Dedicated OT firewalls at zone boundaries (not shared with IT). OT firewall rule set based on documented process communication requirements — whitelist approach. All traffic not explicitly permitted is denied.
Email and general internet access must not be available from OT workstations. HMI/EWS/SCADA systems must not have browser access to internet or IT email. Segregation enforced at firewall.
OT application segmentation: SCADA historian should not run on the same server as SCADA server application. Safety system applications must run on dedicated hardware isolated from non-safety systems.

mappings.frDescription: Respond to security violations and incidents appropriately.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 OT incident response considerations: OT incident response must not blindly apply IT playbooks — isolating a compromised PLC without coordinating with operations can cause safety incidents. Operations engineering must be involved in all OT incident containment decisions. NACSA s26 (6-hour notification) creates a hard deadline that requires a pre-practised response procedure.

nist80082Sections: AU-6 Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting
AU-7 Audit Record Reduction and Report Generation
srs.nist80082OTNote: NIST 800-82 recommends centralised SIEM for OT audit logs. Log correlation across OT and IT enables detection of cross-boundary attacks. OT SIEM requires OT-specific parsers and detection rules.

Passive OT network monitoring (Claroty/Dragos/Nozomi or open-source equivalent) provides continuous visibility without active scanning risk. NIST 800-82 recommends establishing OT network baselines and alerting on deviations.

mappings.frDescription: The IACS maintains availability of all resources required for normal operation. Denial of service is prevented.
mappings.nist80082OTGuidance: NIST 800-82 recognises availability as the primary OT security priority in most operational contexts — OT must keep running even under degraded conditions. Availability controls: backup/recovery, patch management without operational disruption, change management, and denial of service protection for OT communication protocols.

srs.nist80082OTNote: OT DoS is most commonly caused by IT scanning tools accidentally introduced to OT networks. NIST 800-82: never use active scanning tools (Nessus, etc.) on OT networks without vendor pre-approval. OT protocol rate limiting on firewalls.

NIST 800-82 OT backup specifics: backup must include all recovery artefacts (PLC programs, DCS configs, HMI screens, network configs). Offline backup copy essential — ransomware will target any backup reachable from network. RTO must include process reconstitution time, not just system restore time.

OT recovery uniquely requires plant reconstitution: system restored and re-synchronised with physical plant state before returning to automated control. Operations engineering must lead plant reconstitution — IT cannot do this alone.

OT baseline configurations: document expected state for every OT system (enabled services, listening ports, scheduled tasks, user accounts, firewall rules). Deviation from baseline is a security event. NIST 800-82: OT configuration management must be integrated with the change management process.

OT system hardening: disable all unnecessary services (Windows services, network protocols, running processes) on OT workstations and servers. OT hardening guides available from US-CERT ICS-CERT and from OT vendors. Hardening is a commissioning step, not a post-deployment activity.

Passive OT network discovery (Claroty/Nozomi/Dragos) automates OT asset inventory for connected assets. Manual inventory required for air-gapped and field devices. Inventory must include: make, model, firmware version, IP/MAC, zone, owner, criticality, and patch status.

keyDifferences.iec62443: IEC 62443 is an international standard — compliance is verifiable against defined Security Levels. It is the expected NACSA Act 854 s20 standard for OT NCII in Malaysia.
keyDifferences.nist80082: NIST SP 800-82 is guidance, not a compliance standard. It does not define measurable security levels. It is a reference document for OT security practitioners.

keyDifferences.iec62443: Defines SL 1-4 with specific control requirements per level — provides a structured maturity pathway and measurable compliance target.
keyDifferences.nist80082: Does not define security levels. References NIST SP 800-53 control baselines (Low/Moderate/High) which do not map directly to IEC 62443 SLs.

keyDifferences.iec62443: Purpose-built for Industrial Automation and Control Systems. All 51 SRs are OT-specific.
keyDifferences.nist80082: Adapts IT security controls for OT context. Provides extensive OT-specific tailoring guidance for SP 800-53 controls.

keyDifferences.iec62443: Primary standard for OT security in Malaysia, EU (NIS2), Singapore (OT Cybersecurity Masterplan), and most international industrial sectors.
keyDifferences.nist80082: Primary reference in U.S. federal OT environments. Widely used as reference globally but not a compliance requirement outside U.S. federal context.
